skill.name: workflow-automation
skill.description: Architect durable, reliable workflow automation systems using platforms like Temporal, Inngest, n8n, and AWS Step Functions. Learn patterns for sequential and parallel workflows, idempotency, and error handling.
skill.summary: Expert guidance on workflow automation, durable execution platforms, and orchestration patterns
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The 'backtick execution' detection refers to Markdown code formatting in documentation text, not actual Ruby/shell commands. The 'weak cryptography' detection refers to the word 'execution' in documentation context, not cryptographic implementation. This skill contains only documentation about workflow automation patterns with no executable code, security risks, or prompt injection attempts.

content.value_statement: Stop losing work to network failures and timeouts. Learn to build reliable, fault-tolerant workflows that survive restarts and handle errors gracefully using durable execution platforms.
actual_capabilities: Design sequential and parallel workflow architectures
Implement idempotency patterns for external API calls
Configure error handling and retry strategies with exponential backoff
Choose appropriate workflow platforms (Temporal, Inngest, n8n, Step Functions)
Structure workflows for observability and debugging
Avoid common anti-patterns like monolithic workflows and side effects
limitations: Provides architectural guidance but cannot execute workflows directly
Does not interact with external workflow platforms or APIs
Cannot debug existing workflow implementations without code context
Platform-specific syntax may require consulting official documentation

use_cases.title: Payment Processing System
use_cases.description: Design fault-tolerant payment workflows that handle network failures, timeout gracefully, and never double-charge customers using idempotency keys.

use_cases.title: Data Pipeline Orchestration
use_cases.description: Coordinate multi-step ETL workflows with parallel processing, error recovery, and checkpoint-based resumption for long-running data jobs.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers working on batch processing

use_cases.title: Microservice Integration
use_cases.description: Implement event-driven workflows that coordinate multiple services with saga patterns for distributed transactions and automatic rollback.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Right Platform
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to build [workflow type] for [use case]. My team has [skill level] experience and priorities are [priorities]. Compare Temporal, Inngest, n8n, and AWS Step Functions for this scenario. Recommend the best fit and explain the tradeoffs.
prompt_templates.scenario: When selecting a workflow automation platform for a new project

prompt_templates.title: Design Idempotent Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm building [workflow type] that calls [external service/API]. How should I implement idempotency? Show me the pattern for generating and validating idempotency keys, and explain where to store them.
prompt_templates.scenario: When preventing duplicate operations in external API calls

prompt_templates.title: Structure Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a retry strategy for [operation type] that may fail with [error types]. Configure exponential backoff, max retries, and fallback behavior. Show me how to structure this in [platform name].
prompt_templates.scenario: When implementing resilient error handling for external dependencies

prompt_templates.title: Break Down Monolithic Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a single workflow that does [complex process]. It's hard to debug and frequently restarts from the beginning. Help me break this into smaller, checkpointed steps with durable state between them.
prompt_templates.scenario: When refactoring large, failure-prone workflows into manageable pieces

output_examples.input: Compare Temporal vs Inngest for a payment processing system written in TypeScript
output_examples.output: For a TypeScript payment system, I recommend Inngest for faster development. Temporal offers more control but requires infrastructure management. Inngest provides managed reliability with SDK-based development. Key tradeoffs: Temporal handles complex state machine workflows better, while Inngest excels at event-driven simplicity. Both provide durable execution, but Inngest has faster developer onboarding.

output_examples.input: How do I prevent charging a customer twice when my workflow retries?
output_examples.output: Always generate a unique idempotency key before the first payment attempt. Send this key with every payment request to your payment processor. If the workflow retries with the same key, the processor will recognize the duplicate and return the original result instead of charging again. Store the key with your transaction record for idempotency period (typically 24-48 hours).

output_examples.input: My n8n workflow keeps timing out when calling slow APIs
output_examples.output: Never call slow APIs directly from your workflow. Instead, use the orchestrator-worker pattern: your workflow dispatches a job to a worker service, which makes the API call asynchronously. The worker posts the result back via webhook or event. Set timeouts on all HTTP nodes (typically 30-60 seconds). For very slow operations, implement polling with exponential backoff intervals.

best_practices: Always use idempotency keys for external API calls to prevent duplicate operations on retry
Set explicit timeouts on all activities and external service calls to prevent hanging workflows
Break long workflows into small steps with checkpointed state for faster recovery from failures
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for retries to avoid overwhelming downstream services
anti_patterns: Do not perform direct I/O operations or side effects in workflow code—always delegate to activities or workers
Never build monolithic workflows that try to do everything in one place; they become impossible to debug and retry efficiently
Avoid passing large data payloads as workflow arguments—store data externally and pass references instead

faq.question: What is durable execution and why do I need it?
faq.answer: Durable execution means your workflow state survives process restarts, network failures, and crashes. The system automatically retries failed steps and resumes from the last successful checkpoint. This eliminates the need to rebuild failed workflows from scratch, reducing on-call burden and data loss.

faq.question: Should I use Temporal, Inngest, n8n, or AWS Step Functions?
faq.answer: Choose Temporal for complex stateful workflows with maximum control. Choose Inngest for event-driven applications with fast development. Choose n8n for business process automation without coding. Choose AWS Step Functions when already in the AWS ecosystem with simple orchestration needs. The best choice depends on your team skills, complexity requirements, and infrastructure preferences.

faq.question: How do I prevent duplicate operations when workflows retry?
faq.answer: Implement idempotency by generating a unique key before any external operation. Send this key with every API request, database write, or payment charge. The downstream service checks if the key was already processed and returns the cached result instead of performing the operation again. Store idempotency keys for at least your retry window duration.

faq.question: What is exponential backoff with jitter?
faq.answer: Exponential backoff increases retry delay after each failure (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s). Jitter adds randomness to these intervals to prevent thundering herd problems where multiple retries synchronize and overwhelm services. Most platforms provide built-in retry policies—use them instead of writing your own sleep loops.

faq.question: Can workflows call external APIs directly?
faq.answer: Technically yes, but it creates fragility. API timeouts will hang your workflow. Instead, use activity functions or workers that make HTTP calls asynchronously with proper timeouts. Your workflow dispatches the work and waits for a completion event or webhook. This separation keeps workflows responsive and resilient to downstream failures.

faq.question: How do I debug failed workflows?
faq.answer: All workflow platforms provide execution histories showing each step, input/output, and failure reasons. Use structured logging in your activity code. Set descriptive names for each workflow step. For complex failures, replay the execution with debug logging enabled. Most platforms also offer tracing integration and query APIs for inspecting running workflow state.
